stem: What is the initial RF pulse in a conventional inversion-recovery sequence?

options.text: A frequency-encoding gradient with no RF

explanation: Inversion recovery begins by flipping longitudinal magnetization through 180 degrees. After the inversion time, an excitation pulse samples the tissue-dependent recovery.

stem: When is a tissue nulled in an inversion-recovery sequence?

options.text: The echo occurs after all tissues complete T2 decay
The receiver bandwidth excludes the tissue's proton density
The tissue reaches maximum positive longitudinal magnetization
The excitation pulse occurs when that tissue's longitudinal magnetization crosses zero

explanation: At the zero crossing, the tissue has no longitudinal component to tip into the transverse plane, so it produces little signal. TI is selected according to the target tissue's T1.

stem: Which tissue is intentionally suppressed by short-tau inversion recovery?
explanation: STIR uses a short inversion time chosen to place fat at its longitudinal null. It provides robust fat suppression even when frequency-selective methods are degraded by field inhomogeneity.

stem: Which material is intentionally suppressed by fluid-attenuated inversion recovery?
explanation: FLAIR uses a long inversion time to null CSF while retaining T2 weighting in brain tissue. Lesions with prolonged T2 can remain bright next to dark CSF.

stem: Why is STIR generally avoided for selective fat suppression after gadolinium administration?

options.text: Gadolinium removes all hydrogen from fat
STIR requires a receiver bandwidth of zero
It nulls tissue by T1 rather than chemical identity and can suppress enhancing tissue
Gadolinium makes fat and water precess at identical frequencies

explanation: STIR suppresses any tissue whose T1 reaches the chosen null, not fat specifically. Gadolinium shortens T1, so enhancing lesions may lose signal and become less conspicuous.

stem: What is a typical cost of inversion-recovery preparation?

options.text: Mandatory phase wrap in every direction
Longer acquisition and reduced signal efficiency

explanation: The inversion pulse and recovery delay add time, and the target tissue is deliberately suppressed. In return, inversion recovery provides strong T1-based contrast and selective nulling.
